---
title: "Order Statistics and the Uniform Distribution of Null p-Values"
subtitle: "Core Sample · Probability Foundations"
---

```{python}
#| label: setup
#| include: false
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from scipy import stats
from math import comb, factorial

# A consistent, restrained visual style for every figure in this article.
plt.rcParams.update({
    "figure.figsize": (6.2, 4.0),
    "figure.dpi": 140,
    "axes.spines.top": False,
    "axes.spines.right": False,
    "axes.grid": True,
    "grid.alpha": 0.25,
    "font.size": 11,
    "axes.titlesize": 12,
    "lines.linewidth": 2.0,
})

POINT  = "#2b3a67"     # data / samples
LINE   = "#c1121f"     # the theoretical curve
ACCENT = "#0a7d4d"     # highlights / a special order statistic
GREY   = "#6c757d"
FILL   = "#9fb3d1"     # shaded areas / histogram bars
```

> **Where this sits in the track.** This is the closing article of the Probability
> Foundations track, the second of the two "sampling-theory" articles that the data
> tracks consume directly (its sibling is [PROB-22 *The Empirical CDF and Sampling With
> Replacement*]). It assumes you can write down a **probability density** and integrate
> it ([PROB-4 *Continuous Random Variables*]), that you can **count** selections and
> arrangements ([PROB-10 *Counting: Permutations and Combinations*]), and that you have
> met the **Beta distribution** as the model for a random proportion on $[0,1]$
> ([PROB-14 *Gamma, Exponential & Beta*]). It also leans lightly on **independence and
> the i.i.d. sample** ([PROB-8]). What it sets up lives mostly in other tracks: the
> uniform-null-p-value fact derived here is the engine of **multiple-comparison
> procedures** in Statistical Inference (INFR D5, by explicit request), and the order
> statistics are the raw material of every quantile estimate, every Q–Q plot, and the
> extreme-value theory that the track flags but does not build.

## Abstract {.unnumbered}

Sort an i.i.d. sample $X_1,\dots,X_n$ from smallest to largest and you get the **order
statistics** $X_{(1)}\le X_{(2)}\le\cdots\le X_{(n)}$ — the sample minimum, the maximum,
and everything between, including the median. This article answers two questions that
turn out to be the *same* question. First: what is the distribution of the $k$-th smallest
value? We derive its exact density by a pure counting argument that pays off the
combinatorics of [PROB-10]. Second: why is a correctly computed p-value **uniformly
distributed on $(0,1)$ under the null hypothesis**? The bridge between them is one short,
beautiful fact — the **probability integral transform**, $F(X)\sim\mathrm{Uniform}(0,1)$
— which also collapses the order statistics of *any* continuous distribution onto the
order statistics of the uniform, and ties those to the Beta distribution of [PROB-14].
The single claim this article defends is that **sorting, the uniform null p-value, and
the Beta distribution are three views of one structure**, and that seeing them as one is
what lets you read a Q–Q plot, calibrate a test, and run a multiple-comparison correction
without it feeling like a bag of unrelated tricks.

# The Core {#sec-core}

::: {.callout-important title="The Core"}
Push any continuous random variable $X$ through its own cumulative distribution function
$F$ and you always get the same thing: $U=F(X)$ is **uniform on $(0,1)$**, no matter what
$F$ was. This one fact — the **probability integral transform** — does double duty. It
makes a valid p-value uniform under the null (a p-value *is* a tail probability of the
test statistic, i.e. an $F$ evaluated at the data), and it reduces the order statistics of
*every* continuous distribution to the order statistics of the uniform. Those uniform
order statistics have an exact, clean distribution: the $k$-th smallest of $n$ draws is
$\mathrm{Beta}(k,\,n-k+1)$. Sorting, calibration, and the Beta law are the same picture.
:::

Everything below orbits that box. The $k$-th order statistic's density will look, at
first, like an intimidating product of an $F$, an $f$, and a binomial-style coefficient —
but by the end you should read it the way you read a sentence: *pick which observation
lands at the value, force $k-1$ of the rest below it and $n-k$ above it, and count the
ways to do that.* And the uniform p-value is not a separate theorem to memorize; it is the
probability integral transform wearing a lab coat.

# Why we are here: two problems that were really one {#sec-why}

Keep one question in view for the whole article: **what happens to a sample when we
sort it, and why does that explain the calibration of a hypothesis test?** Every
derivation below answers a piece of that. If a step feels arbitrary, come back here.

## Problem one: the tyranny of the extreme

Sorting is the oldest data operation there is, and for most of statistics' history the
*ends* of a sorted sample mattered more than the middle. A civil engineer sizing a levee
does not care about the average annual flood; she cares about the **largest** flood in a
hundred years — the maximum, $X_{(n)}$. An insurer pricing catastrophe cover cares about
the worst loss, not the typical one. A chip fabricator cares about the **weakest** link in
a circuit — the minimum strength, $X_{(1)}$ — because that is where it breaks. The median
$X_{((n+1)/2)}$ is the robust center that a single wild value cannot drag around, the
reason we report median household income rather than mean. Each of these is an **order
statistic**: a specific position in the sorted sample. To reason about any of them — to put
a probability on "the worst flood exceeds the levee" — we need the *distribution* of a
chosen rank, not of a raw observation. That is the first half of the article.

## Problem two: what does a p-value mean when nothing is going on?

The second problem comes from the other end of statistics, the testing tradition that
Fisher, Neyman, and Pearson built in the 1920s and 30s. You run an experiment, compute a
**p-value**, and the rule is: reject the null hypothesis if $p$ is small, say below
$0.05$. But that rule only makes sense if we know how $p$ behaves *when the null is true*
— when there is genuinely no effect and the small $p$ would be a false alarm. The whole
edifice of "a 5% test rejects a true null 5% of the time" rests on a claim that, under the
null, the p-value is **uniformly distributed on $(0,1)$**: every value in $(0,1)$ is
equally likely, so $P(p\le 0.05)=0.05$ exactly. Where does that uniformity come from? It is
not an assumption we impose; it is forced, and the thing that forces it is the same
transform that lets us reduce all order statistics to uniform ones. The two problems —
sorting a sample and calibrating a test — share an engine.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"Why does a hypothesis test even have a 'distribution of p-values'? Isn't a p-value just a
number?"* For one fixed dataset it is one number. But the data are random — run the
experiment again and you would get a different sample and a different p-value. So $p$ is a
*function of the random data*, hence itself a random variable, with its own distribution.
The calibration claim is a statement about that distribution, under the assumption that the
null hypothesis generated the data. Confusing the single observed $p$ with its distribution
is the root of most p-value misunderstandings, and we settle it in @sec-pvalue.
:::

# Intuition before symbols {#sec-intuition}

Picture $n$ points dropped at random on a line — say $n=5$ draws from some continuous
distribution. They land in some scrambled order: maybe the third one you drew is actually
the smallest, the first is the largest. **Sorting relabels them by position.** The order
statistic $X_{(k)}$ is "whoever ended up in slot $k$ from the left," not "the $k$-th one I
drew." @fig-sorting shows one sample, raw and sorted, so the relabelling is concrete.

```{python}
#| label: fig-sorting
#| fig-cap: "Sorting relabels a sample by rank. **Top:** five i.i.d. draws in the order they arrived, $X_1,\\dots,X_5$. **Bottom:** the same five values placed on the axis; reading left to right gives the order statistics $X_{(1)}\\le\\cdots\\le X_{(5)}$. The minimum $X_{(1)}$ (green) and maximum $X_{(5)}$ (red) are the extremes; $X_{(3)}$ is the sample median. Which *original* draw lands in which slot is itself random."
rng = np.random.default_rng(3)
draws = rng.normal(5, 1.6, size=5)
order = np.argsort(draws)

fig, (axT, axB) = plt.subplots(2, 1, figsize=(7.2, 3.4), sharex=True)
axT.scatter(draws, np.zeros_like(draws), color=POINT, s=80, zorder=3)
for i, d in enumerate(draws):
    axT.annotate(fr"$X_{{{i+1}}}$", (d, 0), textcoords="offset points",
                 xytext=(0, 10), ha="center", fontsize=11)
axT.set_yticks([]); axT.set_title("As drawn (arrival order)")
axT.set_ylim(-0.5, 0.8)

srt = np.sort(draws)
colors = [ACCENT] + [POINT]*3 + [LINE]
for k, (v, c) in enumerate(zip(srt, colors), start=1):
    axB.scatter([v], [0], color=c, s=80, zorder=3)
    axB.annotate(fr"$X_{{({k})}}$", (v, 0), textcoords="offset points",
                 xytext=(0, 10), ha="center", fontsize=11)
axB.set_yticks([]); axB.set_title("Sorted (order statistics)")
axB.set_ylim(-0.5, 0.8); axB.set_xlabel("value")
plt.tight_layout()
```

Two things are worth feeling before we compute anything.

**The extremes are pulled toward the tails, and they bunch.** The maximum of $n$ draws is,
on average, further out than any single draw — and the more draws, the further out it
goes. The minimum is symmetric. So even if each $X_i$ is a tame bell curve, $X_{(n)}$ lives
out in the right tail and has its *own*, skewed distribution. The middle order statistics,
by contrast, concentrate tightly around the population median.

**For the uniform distribution, the order statistics spread out evenly.** If you drop $n$
uniform points on $[0,1]$, they tend to space themselves at roughly $1/(n+1)$, $2/(n+1)$,
…, $n/(n+1)$ — the line gets cut into $n+1$ pieces of equal *expected* length. That regular
spacing is the visual seed of the Beta distribution we will derive, and (via the
probability integral transform) it is the same regularity that makes a column of null
p-values lie on the diagonal of a Q–Q plot.

# The formal development {#sec-formal}

## Setting up: the sample and its sorted version {#sec-setup}

::: {#def-order-stat}
**Definition (order statistics).** Let $X_1,X_2,\dots,X_n$ be i.i.d. random variables with
common cumulative distribution function (CDF) $F(x)=P(X\le x)$ and, where it exists,
probability density function (pdf) $f(x)=F'(x)$. The **order statistics** are the same
values sorted into nondecreasing order, written
$$
X_{(1)}\le X_{(2)}\le\cdots\le X_{(n)},
$$
so that $X_{(1)}=\min_i X_i$ is the sample minimum, $X_{(n)}=\max_i X_i$ is the sample
maximum, and $X_{(k)}$ is the $k$-th smallest value.
:::

Two notational reminders, because the subscripts do a lot of work. The plain subscript in
$X_i$ marks **arrival order** (the $i$-th observation collected); the *parenthesized*
subscript in $X_{(k)}$ marks **rank** (the $k$-th smallest). They are different random
variables. "i.i.d." abbreviates *independent and identically distributed*: the $X_i$ are
drawn independently, each from the same distribution $F$ — the standard model of a clean
random sample, built in [PROB-8].

We will assume throughout that $X$ is **continuous**, i.e. $F$ has a density $f$. This buys
us a small but crucial simplification: for a continuous variable, $P(X_i=X_j)=0$ for $i\ne
j$, so **ties never happen** (with probability one). Every draw is distinct, the sort is
unambiguous, and the $\le$ signs above can be read as strict $<$. (The discrete case, with
its ties, is genuinely messier; we flag it in @sec-failure and stay continuous in the
derivations.)

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"Are the order statistics independent?"* No — emphatically not, even though the original
$X_i$ are. Sorting introduces dependence by construction: knowing $X_{(1)}=3.2$ tells you
$X_{(2)}\ge 3.2$, so the order statistics are forced into a chain $X_{(1)}\le X_{(2)}\le
\cdots$. They are a textbook example of variables that are dependent *because of a
deterministic constraint*, not because the underlying randomness is correlated. This is why
their joint distribution (@sec-joint) carries an $n!$ and is supported only on the ordered
region, and it is the single most common interview trap on this topic.
:::

## The CDF of a single order statistic {#sec-cdf-orderstat}

We want the distribution of one chosen rank, $X_{(k)}$. The cleanest path to its **density**
goes through its **CDF**, because the CDF of an order statistic is a counting event we can
write down directly. Fix a value $x$ and ask: what does it *mean* for the $k$-th smallest
to be at most $x$?

The event $\{X_{(k)}\le x\}$ says the $k$-th smallest value sits at or below $x$. The
$k$-th smallest is below $x$ **exactly when at least $k$ of the $n$ observations are below
$x$** — if $k$ or more of them fall in $(-\infty,x]$, then in particular the $k$-th from
the bottom does. Conversely, if fewer than $k$ fall below $x$, the $k$-th smallest must lie
above $x$. So the two events are identical:
$$
\{X_{(k)}\le x\} \;=\; \{\text{at least } k \text{ of the } X_i \text{ are} \le x\}.
$$ {#eq-orderstat-event}

This is the whole trick, and it converts an order-statistic question into a **counting**
question, which is where [PROB-10] pays off. Define, for the fixed threshold $x$, an
indicator for each observation:
$$
B_i = \mathbf{1}\{X_i\le x\}=\begin{cases}1 & X_i\le x,\\[2pt] 0 & X_i> x.\end{cases}
$$
Each $B_i$ is a **Bernoulli trial**: it succeeds (equals 1) with probability
$P(X_i\le x)=F(x)$ and fails with probability $1-F(x)$, and because the $X_i$ are
independent, so are the $B_i$. Let $N_x=\sum_{i=1}^n B_i$ be the number of observations at
or below $x$. A sum of $n$ independent Bernoulli$(p)$ trials is, by definition, a
**Binomial$(n,p)$** variable (this is exactly the construction in [PROB-11]), here with
success probability $p=F(x)$:
$$
N_x \sim \mathrm{Binomial}\bigl(n,\,F(x)\bigr),
\qquad
P(N_x=j)=\binom{n}{j}F(x)^j\bigl(1-F(x)\bigr)^{n-j}.
$$ {#eq-binomial-count}

Now translate @eq-orderstat-event through $N_x$: "at least $k$ observations $\le x$" is
just $\{N_x\ge k\}$. Therefore the CDF of the $k$-th order statistic is the upper tail of
this binomial:
$$
F_{(k)}(x) \;=\; P\bigl(X_{(k)}\le x\bigr)
\;=\; P\bigl(N_x\ge k\bigr)
\;=\; \sum_{j=k}^{n}\binom{n}{j}F(x)^j\bigl(1-F(x)\bigr)^{n-j}.
$$ {#eq-orderstat-cdf}

Two important special cases fall straight out, and they are worth doing by hand because
they are the ones you will actually use.

**The maximum, $X_{(n)}$.** The largest value is $\le x$ precisely when *every*
observation is $\le x$. With $k=n$, @eq-orderstat-cdf has only the $j=n$ term,
$\binom{n}{n}F(x)^n(1-F(x))^0=F(x)^n$. Or, even more directly, by independence,
$$
F_{(n)}(x)=P\bigl(\max_i X_i\le x\bigr)
=P\bigl(X_1\le x,\dots,X_n\le x\bigr)
=\prod_{i=1}^n P(X_i\le x)=F(x)^n.
$$ {#eq-max-cdf}

**The minimum, $X_{(1)}$.** It is cleaner to work with the complement. The smallest value
*exceeds* $x$ precisely when *every* observation exceeds $x$:
$$
P\bigl(X_{(1)}> x\bigr)=P\bigl(X_1> x,\dots,X_n> x\bigr)=\prod_{i=1}^n\bigl(1-F(x)\bigr)
=\bigl(1-F(x)\bigr)^n,
$$
so the CDF of the minimum is
$$
F_{(1)}(x)=1-P\bigl(X_{(1)}> x\bigr)=1-\bigl(1-F(x)\bigr)^n.
$$ {#eq-min-cdf}

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"Quick — if I draw $n$ values uniformly on $[0,1]$, what's the probability the maximum is
below $0.9$?"* Use @eq-max-cdf with $F(x)=x$ on $[0,1]$: $P(X_{(n)}\le 0.9)=0.9^{\,n}$. For
$n=10$ that is $0.9^{10}\approx 0.349$, so there is about a 65% chance at least one of ten
uniform draws lands in the top tenth. This is the "max is pulled toward the tail"
intuition of @sec-intuition made numerical, and it is the kind of one-liner an interviewer
likes precisely because the independence-product derivation is so short.
:::

## From CDF to density: the counting derivation {#sec-density}

The CDF in @eq-orderstat-cdf is exact but awkward to differentiate as written (it is a sum
of many terms). There is a far more illuminating route to the density of $X_{(k)}$ — a
direct counting argument that makes the formula's structure transparent. We build it as a
**multinomial** count, which is the natural generalization of the binomial coefficient from
[PROB-10] to three categories.

We want $f_{(k)}(x)$, the density of $X_{(k)}$ at the value $x$. Recall what a density means
(from [PROB-4]): $f_{(k)}(x)\,dx$ is, to first order, the probability that $X_{(k)}$ lands
in a tiny window $[x,x+dx]$. So we ask: in what configurations of the $n$ original draws
does the $k$-th smallest land in $[x,x+dx]$?

For the $k$-th order statistic to sit in that sliver, the $n$ observations must split into
**three groups**:

1. Exactly **one** observation lands *inside* the window $[x,x+dx]$ — that one will be
   $X_{(k)}$ itself.
2. Exactly **$k-1$** observations land strictly *below* $x$ — they are the ones ranked
   $1,\dots,k-1$, forced beneath our value.
3. The remaining **$n-k$** observations land strictly *above* $x+dx$ — ranked
   $k+1,\dots,n$, forced above.

(We may ignore the vanishingly small chance that two observations land in the same tiny
window, because for a continuous distribution that probability is of order $(dx)^2$, which
disappears relative to the $dx$ we are tracking.) Now we attach a probability to each group
and count the ways to assign the $n$ labelled observations to the groups.

**The probability of each group's placement.** For a single observation, drawn from $F$:

- the chance it falls below $x$ is $P(X\le x)=F(x)$;
- the chance it falls in the window $[x,x+dx]$ is, by the definition of the density,
  approximately $f(x)\,dx$ (the area under the density over a width-$dx$ strip);
- the chance it falls above $x+dx$ is $P(X> x)=1-F(x)$ (and $1-F(x+dx)\to 1-F(x)$ as
  $dx\to0$, so we write $1-F(x)$).

Because the $n$ observations are independent, the probability of one *specific* assignment
— these particular $k-1$ observations below, this particular one in the window, those
particular $n-k$ above — is the product
$$
\underbrace{F(x)^{\,k-1}}_{k-1\text{ below}}\;\cdot\;
\underbrace{f(x)\,dx}_{1\text{ in window}}\;\cdot\;
\underbrace{\bigl(1-F(x)\bigr)^{\,n-k}}_{n-k\text{ above}}.
$$

**The count of assignments.** But *which* observations go where is itself something we must
count, because the observations are distinguishable (observation 1 is not observation 2)
and any of them could be the one that lands in the window. The number of ways to choose
which of the $n$ labelled observations form the "below" group of size $k-1$, the "window"
group of size $1$, and the "above" group of size $n-k$ is the **multinomial coefficient**
$$
\binom{n}{\,k-1,\;1,\;n-k\,}=\frac{n!}{(k-1)!\,1!\,(n-k)!}.
$$ {#eq-multinomial}

This is the three-category generalization of the binomial coefficient: $\binom{n}{a,b,c}$
counts the ways to deal $n$ distinct items into groups of sizes $a,b,c$ (with
$a+b+c=n$), and it equals $n!/(a!\,b!\,c!)$. (When there are only two groups it collapses
to the familiar $\binom{n}{k}=n!/(k!\,(n-k)!)$ of [PROB-10]; the multinomial is the same
idea with one more bin.) We derive @eq-multinomial inline in @sec-multinomial-derivation
below so nothing is taken on faith.

**Putting it together.** Multiply the count by the probability of one assignment, and
divide by $dx$ to turn a probability-of-a-window into a density:
$$
\boxed{\;
f_{(k)}(x)=\frac{n!}{(k-1)!\,(n-k)!}\;F(x)^{\,k-1}\,\bigl(1-F(x)\bigr)^{\,n-k}\,f(x).
\;}
$$ {#eq-orderstat-density}

(The $1!=1$ from the window group is invisible, so we drop it.) Read the boxed formula as a
sentence, exactly as promised in the Core: the coefficient counts *who goes where*;
$F(x)^{k-1}$ forces $k-1$ observations below; $(1-F(x))^{n-k}$ forces $n-k$ above; and
$f(x)$ places the lone chosen observation *at* $x$. Every factor has a job.

### Filling the one gap: where the multinomial coefficient comes from {#sec-multinomial-derivation}

We used @eq-multinomial; here is its derivation so no step is implicit. We have $n$
distinct observations and want to split them into an ordered list of bins of sizes
$k-1$, $1$, and $n-k$. Build the split in stages and use the multiplication principle of
[PROB-10]. First choose which $k-1$ observations go below: that is $\binom{n}{k-1}$ ways.
From the $n-(k-1)$ that remain, choose the single one for the window: $\binom{n-k+1}{1}$
ways. The last $n-k$ are forced into the "above" bin: $\binom{n-k}{n-k}=1$ way. By the
multiplication principle the total is the product
$$
\binom{n}{k-1}\binom{n-k+1}{1}\binom{n-k}{n-k}
=\frac{n!}{(k-1)!\,(n-k+1)!}\cdot\frac{(n-k+1)!}{1!\,(n-k)!}\cdot 1.
$$
The $(n-k+1)!$ cancels, leaving $\dfrac{n!}{(k-1)!\,1!\,(n-k)!}$, which is exactly
@eq-multinomial. $\quad\blacksquare$

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"Can you get the density by differentiating the CDF instead, and do the two agree?"* Yes,
and checking is a good way to trust the counting argument. Differentiate @eq-orderstat-cdf
term by term in $x$ (using the chain rule, $\frac{d}{dx}F(x)=f(x)$). The sum
$\sum_{j=k}^n\binom{n}{j}F^j(1-F)^{n-j}$ telescopes spectacularly: almost everything
cancels and you are left with precisely the single term in @eq-orderstat-density. We carry
out that cancellation in full in @sec-telescoping for the curious; the upshot is that the
combinatorial derivation and the calculus derivation give the identical formula, as they
must.
:::

## A quick sanity check: the density integrates to one {#sec-sanity}

A density must integrate to $1$ over its support ([PROB-4]). Let us confirm
@eq-orderstat-density does, which also rehearses a substitution we will reuse. Integrate
over all $x$:
$$
\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} f_{(k)}(x)\,dx
=\frac{n!}{(k-1)!\,(n-k)!}\int_{-\infty}^{\infty}
   F(x)^{\,k-1}\bigl(1-F(x)\bigr)^{\,n-k} f(x)\,dx.
$$
Substitute $u=F(x)$, so $du=f(x)\,dx$ (the chain rule again, from [PROB-4]). As $x$ runs
from $-\infty$ to $\infty$, $u=F(x)$ runs from $0$ to $1$ (a CDF rises from $0$ to $1$).
The integral becomes
$$
\frac{n!}{(k-1)!\,(n-k)!}\int_{0}^{1} u^{\,k-1}(1-u)^{\,n-k}\,du.
$$
That remaining integral is the **Beta function** $B(k,\,n-k+1)=\int_0^1 u^{k-1}(1-u)^{n-k}\,du$,
and from [PROB-14] we know $B(\alpha,\beta)=\Gamma(\alpha)\Gamma(\beta)/\Gamma(\alpha+\beta)$.
For integer arguments $\Gamma(m)=(m-1)!$, so
$$
B(k,\,n-k+1)=\frac{\Gamma(k)\,\Gamma(n-k+1)}{\Gamma(n+1)}
=\frac{(k-1)!\,(n-k)!}{n!}.
$$
This is exactly the reciprocal of the coefficient out front, so the product is $1$. The
density is legitimate — and, not by accident, the Beta function just walked on stage. Hold
that thought; it becomes the headline result in @sec-uniform-beta.

## The minimum, the maximum, and the median made explicit {#sec-special}

With @eq-orderstat-density in hand, the named order statistics are just substitutions.

**Minimum ($k=1$).** Then $k-1=0$ and $n-k=n-1$, and $\frac{n!}{0!\,(n-1)!}=n$, giving
$$
f_{(1)}(x)=n\,\bigl(1-F(x)\bigr)^{\,n-1} f(x).
$$ {#eq-min-density}
Differentiating @eq-min-cdf directly gives the same thing:
$\frac{d}{dx}\!\left[1-(1-F)^n\right]=n(1-F)^{n-1}f$. The two routes agree.

**Maximum ($k=n$).** Then $k-1=n-1$ and $n-k=0$, and $\frac{n!}{(n-1)!\,0!}=n$, giving
$$
f_{(n)}(x)=n\,F(x)^{\,n-1} f(x),
$$ {#eq-max-density}
which is the derivative of $F(x)^n$ from @eq-max-cdf. Agreement again.

**Median.** When $n=2m+1$ is odd, the sample median is the middle order statistic,
$X_{(m+1)}$, with $k-1=m$ below and $n-k=m$ above. Its density is the symmetric
$$
f_{(m+1)}(x)=\frac{(2m+1)!}{m!\,m!}\,F(x)^{m}\bigl(1-F(x)\bigr)^{m} f(x),
$$
heavily concentrated near the population median (where $F=1/2$), because $F^m(1-F)^m$ peaks
sharply at $F=1/2$ as $m$ grows. @fig-order-densities draws these densities for a standard
normal sample, making the "extremes flee to the tails, the median concentrates" picture of
@sec-intuition quantitative.

```{python}
#| label: fig-order-densities
#| fig-cap: "Densities of selected order statistics for $n=9$ i.i.d. standard-normal draws, from @eq-orderstat-density with $F=\\Phi$, $f=\\varphi$. The minimum $X_{(1)}$ (green) sits in the left tail and the maximum $X_{(9)}$ (red) in the right; both are skewed. The median $X_{(5)}$ (dark) is tight and symmetric around $0$. A single raw draw's density (grey dashed) is shown for scale. Sorting manufactures a whole family of distributions from one parent."
from scipy.stats import norm

n = 9
xs = np.linspace(-3.5, 3.5, 600)
F = norm.cdf(xs)
f = norm.pdf(xs)

def order_density(k, n, F, f):
    coef = factorial(n) / (factorial(k-1) * factorial(n-k))
    return coef * F**(k-1) * (1 - F)**(n-k) * f

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7.0, 4.2))
ax.plot(xs, f, color=GREY, ls="--", lw=1.4, label="single draw  $\\varphi(x)$")
ax.plot(xs, order_density(1, n, F, f), color=ACCENT, label="min  $X_{(1)}$")
ax.plot(xs, order_density(3, n, F, f), color="#7048a8", label="$X_{(3)}$")
ax.plot(xs, order_density(5, n, F, f), color=POINT, label="median  $X_{(5)}$")
ax.plot(xs, order_density(7, n, F, f), color="#c97a00", label="$X_{(7)}$")
ax.plot(xs, order_density(9, n, F, f), color=LINE, label="max  $X_{(9)}$")
ax.set_xlabel("$x$"); ax.set_ylabel("density")
ax.set_title("Order-statistic densities, $n=9$ standard normals")
ax.legend(loc="upper right", fontsize=8.5, framealpha=0.9)
plt.tight_layout()
```

## The joint density of all the order statistics {#sec-joint}

For completeness — and because the uniform-spacing intuition needs it — here is the
*joint* density of the whole sorted vector, derived by the same counting logic. Suppose we
want the chance that the sorted values land near a specific increasing sequence
$y_1<y_2<\cdots<y_n$. The original sample, *as drawn*, has joint density
$\prod_{i=1}^n f(x_i)$ (independence multiplies densities, [PROB-8]). But the *sorted*
sample at $(y_1,\dots,y_n)$ could have come from any of the $n!$ orderings of those same
values — observation 1 might have been any of the $n$ slots, and so on — and each
permutation has the same density product. So we multiply by $n!$:
$$
f_{X_{(1)},\dots,X_{(n)}}(y_1,\dots,y_n)=n!\,\prod_{i=1}^n f(y_i),
\qquad y_1<y_2<\cdots<y_n,
$$ {#eq-joint-density}
and the density is zero off the ordered region $y_1<\cdots<y_n$. The lone factor $n!$ is
the entire effect of sorting: it concentrates all the probability mass that was spread over
$n!$ symmetric orderings onto the single ordered one. This is the precise sense in which
sorting *creates dependence* (the "You might be asked" box of @sec-setup): the support is
the constrained wedge $y_1<\cdots<y_n$, not the full cube.

# The bridge: the probability integral transform {#sec-pit}

Everything so far worked for a *general* continuous $F$. We now prove the single fact that
collapses all of it onto the uniform distribution, and that simultaneously explains null
p-values. It is short. It is the most important thing in the article.

::: {#thm-pit}
**Theorem (probability integral transform, PIT).** Let $X$ be a continuous random variable
with CDF $F$. Define the new random variable $U=F(X)$ — that is, run $X$ through its own
CDF. Then $U$ is **uniformly distributed on $(0,1)$**: $P(U\le u)=u$ for all $u\in[0,1]$,
regardless of what $F$ was.
:::

::: {.proof}
We find the CDF of $U$, i.e. $P(U\le u)$ for a fixed $u\in(0,1)$, and show it equals $u$ —
the defining CDF of a Uniform$(0,1)$ variable (from [PROB-4], the Uniform$(0,1)$ has
$P(U\le u)=u$ on $[0,1]$).

Start from the definition $U=F(X)$:
$$
P(U\le u)=P\bigl(F(X)\le u\bigr).
$$
We want to "undo" $F$ on both sides of the inequality inside. For a *strictly increasing*
$F$ this is immediate — apply the inverse $F^{-1}$, which preserves the inequality because
an increasing function preserves order:
$$
P\bigl(F(X)\le u\bigr)=P\bigl(X\le F^{-1}(u)\bigr).
$$
But the right-hand side is just the CDF $F$ evaluated at $F^{-1}(u)$:
$$
P\bigl(X\le F^{-1}(u)\bigr)=F\bigl(F^{-1}(u)\bigr)=u.
$$
So $P(U\le u)=u$ on $(0,1)$, which is the Uniform$(0,1)$ CDF. (And $P(U\le u)=0$ for $u<0$,
$=1$ for $u>1$, since $F$ takes values only in $[0,1]$.) That is the claim. $\quad\blacksquare$
:::

::: {.callout-note title="The one technical caveat, handled honestly"}
The clean proof used $F^{-1}$, which requires $F$ to be *strictly* increasing (so the
inverse is a genuine function). For a general continuous $F$ — one that might be flat on
some interval where the density is zero — there is no ordinary inverse, so we cannot just
"apply $F^{-1}$." We do not have to hand-wave past this; the argument is short, and it
shows precisely where continuity earns its keep.

Define the **generalized inverse** (quantile function)
$F^{-1}(u)=\inf\{x:F(x)\ge u\}$ — the leftmost value the CDF reaches level $u$ at. Write
$x_u=F^{-1}(u)$. Two facts about $x_u$, both from elementary properties of $F$:

1. **$F(x_u)=u$ exactly.** By definition of the infimum there are points $x$ just above
   $x_u$ with $F(x)\ge u$, so right-continuity (a CDF is right-continuous, [PROB-2]) gives
   $F(x_u)\ge u$; and there are points $x<x_u$ with $F(x)<u$, so $F(x_u)\le u$ by
   monotonicity and **continuity** — a jump at $x_u$ could leave $F(x_u)$ strictly above
   $u$, but a *continuous* $F$ cannot skip over the level $u$. Hence $F(x_u)=u$. This is the
   one and only place continuity is used, and it is exactly the step a discrete $F$ breaks.
2. **$\{F(X)\le u\}$ and $\{X\le x_u\}$ differ by at most a probability-zero set.** If
   $x\le x_u$ then $F(x)\le F(x_u)=u$ by monotonicity, so $\{X\le x_u\}\subseteq\{F(X)\le
   u\}$. The reverse can fail only on a *flat* stretch of $F$ at exactly level $u$: a maximal
   interval $(x_u,\,b]$ on which $F\equiv u$, where $F(x)=u\le u$ yet $x>x_u$. But $F$ being
   flat on that interval means the density is zero there, so $P(x_u<X\le b)=F(b)-F(x_u)=u-u=0$
   — the discrepancy set carries no probability. (If $F$ is strictly increasing there is no
   flat stretch and the two events are equal outright.)

Therefore $P(F(X)\le u)=P(X\le x_u)$, and
$$
P(U\le u)=P\bigl(F(X)\le u\bigr)=P\bigl(X\le x_u\bigr)=F(x_u)=u,
$$
recovering the same conclusion as the strictly-increasing case. The honest boundary is the
**discrete** case: if $F$ jumps, fact 1 fails — $F(X)$ can only land on the jump heights, so
it is *not* continuous and *not* uniform. We return to this in @sec-failure, because it is
exactly why p-values from discrete tests are not uniform.
:::

The PIT has a twin, which we will need to *generate* and to *reason backward*: the inverse
direction.

::: {#thm-inverse-pit}
**Theorem (inverse transform).** If $U\sim\mathrm{Uniform}(0,1)$ and $F$ is a CDF with
quantile function $F^{-1}$, then $X=F^{-1}(U)$ has CDF $F$.
:::

::: {.proof}
$P(X\le x)=P\bigl(F^{-1}(U)\le x\bigr)=P\bigl(U\le F(x)\bigr)$ (apply $F$, increasing, to
both sides of the inequality), and since $U$ is uniform, $P(U\le F(x))=F(x)$. So $X$ has
CDF $F$. $\quad\blacksquare$
:::

@thm-inverse-pit is how computers *simulate* any distribution: draw a uniform, push it
through the quantile function. @thm-pit runs that movie backward. Together they say a
continuous distribution and the uniform are interchangeable through the lens of $F$ — a
fact we now cash in twice.

```{python}
#| label: fig-pit
#| fig-cap: "The probability integral transform in action. **Left:** a sample of $n=4000$ from a right-skewed Exponential(1) distribution — anything but uniform. **Right:** push each value through its own CDF, $U=F(X)=1-e^{-X}$. The histogram of the $U$ values is flat — Uniform$(0,1)$ — exactly as @thm-pit guarantees, and *independently of the lopsided shape we started with*."
rng = np.random.default_rng(11)
x = rng.exponential(scale=1.0, size=4000)
u = 1 - np.exp(-x)   # F(x) for Exponential(1)

fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(8.6, 3.6))
axL.hist(x, bins=40, color=FILL, edgecolor="white", density=True)
xs = np.linspace(0, x.max(), 200)
axL.plot(xs, np.exp(-xs), color=LINE, label="Exp(1) density")
axL.set_title("Original $X\\sim$ Exponential(1)")
axL.set_xlabel("$x$"); axL.set_ylabel("density"); axL.legend(fontsize=9)

axR.hist(u, bins=40, color=FILL, edgecolor="white", density=True)
axR.axhline(1.0, color=LINE, lw=2, label="Uniform(0,1) density")
axR.set_title("Transformed $U=F(X)$")
axR.set_xlabel("$u$"); axR.set_ylabel("density")
axR.set_ylim(0, 1.6); axR.legend(fontsize=9)
plt.tight_layout()
```

# Cashing it in, part one: uniform order statistics and the Beta {#sec-uniform-beta}

Apply @eq-orderstat-density to the uniform itself. For $U\sim\mathrm{Uniform}(0,1)$ the CDF
is $F(u)=u$ and the density is $f(u)=1$ on $[0,1]$. Substituting into the boxed density,
$$
f_{U_{(k)}}(u)=\frac{n!}{(k-1)!\,(n-k)!}\,u^{\,k-1}(1-u)^{\,n-k}\cdot 1,
\qquad 0\le u\le 1.
$$ {#eq-uniform-order}

Compare this with the **Beta density** from [PROB-14]: a $\mathrm{Beta}(\alpha,\beta)$
random variable on $[0,1]$ has density
$\dfrac{\Gamma(\alpha+\beta)}{\Gamma(\alpha)\Gamma(\beta)}u^{\alpha-1}(1-u)^{\beta-1}$.
Matching exponents, $\alpha-1=k-1$ and $\beta-1=n-k$, i.e. $\alpha=k$ and $\beta=n-k+1$;
and the constant matches because $\Gamma(n+1)/[\Gamma(k)\Gamma(n-k+1)]=n!/[(k-1)!\,(n-k)!]$
(integer Gammas are factorials, as in @sec-sanity). Therefore:

::: {#thm-uniform-beta}
**Theorem (uniform order statistics are Beta).** If $U_1,\dots,U_n$ are i.i.d.
Uniform$(0,1)$, then the $k$-th order statistic is exactly Beta-distributed:
$$
U_{(k)}\sim\mathrm{Beta}(k,\;n-k+1).
$$
:::

This is a genuinely clean result, and it instantly hands us moments without any new
integration. From [PROB-14], a $\mathrm{Beta}(\alpha,\beta)$ has mean
$\alpha/(\alpha+\beta)$ and variance $\alpha\beta/[(\alpha+\beta)^2(\alpha+\beta+1)]$.
Plugging $\alpha=k,\ \beta=n-k+1$ (so $\alpha+\beta=n+1$):
$$
E\bigl[U_{(k)}\bigr]=\frac{k}{n+1},
\qquad
\mathrm{Var}\bigl(U_{(k)}\bigr)=\frac{k\,(n-k+1)}{(n+1)^2\,(n+2)}.
$$ {#eq-uniform-moments}

The mean $E[U_{(k)}]=k/(n+1)$ is the regular spacing promised in @sec-intuition: $n$
uniform points cut $[0,1]$ into $n+1$ gaps of equal *expected* length $1/(n+1)$, so the
$k$-th point sits, on average, at $k/(n+1)$. (This, not $k/n$, is the right "plotting
position" for a Q–Q plot — a practical payoff we use in @sec-qq.) And because the variance
$k(n-k+1)/[(n+1)^2(n+2)]$ is largest near $k\approx n/2$ and smallest at the extremes
$k=1,n$, the *middle* uniform order statistics are the wiggliest and the *extremes* the
most pinned down — the mirror image of the normal case, a contrast worth carrying.

**And now the general case is free.** Take *any* continuous parent $F$. By the PIT,
$U_i=F(X_i)$ are i.i.d. uniform. Crucially, because $F$ is increasing, **sorting the
$X_i$ sorts the $U_i$ in lockstep**: $U_{(k)}=F(X_{(k)})$. So the order statistics of any
continuous distribution are just $X_{(k)}=F^{-1}(U_{(k)})$ with $U_{(k)}\sim
\mathrm{Beta}(k,n-k+1)$. That is the structural unification of the Core: *every*
continuous order-statistics problem is a Beta variable pushed through a quantile function.

```{python}
#| label: fig-uniform-beta
#| fig-cap: "The Beta law of uniform order statistics, @thm-uniform-beta. For $n=10$ uniform draws we simulate $200{,}000$ replications and histogram three order statistics. Overlaid (red) are the exact $\\mathrm{Beta}(k,\\,n-k+1)$ densities: $U_{(1)}\\sim\\mathrm{Beta}(1,10)$, $U_{(5)}\\sim\\mathrm{Beta}(5,6)$, $U_{(10)}\\sim\\mathrm{Beta}(10,1)$. The simulated histograms sit right on top of the exact Beta curves. Vertical ticks mark the means $k/(n+1)$ from @eq-uniform-moments."
from scipy.stats import beta as beta_dist

rng = np.random.default_rng(7)
n = 10
reps = 200_000
U = np.sort(rng.random((reps, n)), axis=1)

fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 3, figsize=(9.2, 3.3), sharey=True)
for ax, k in zip(axes, (1, 5, 10)):
    ax.hist(U[:, k-1], bins=60, density=True, color=FILL, edgecolor="white")
    uu = np.linspace(0, 1, 300)
    ax.plot(uu, beta_dist.pdf(uu, k, n - k + 1), color=LINE, lw=2)
    ax.axvline(k / (n + 1), color=ACCENT, lw=1.6, ls="--")
    ax.set_title(f"$U_{{({k})}}\\sim$ Beta$({k},{n-k+1})$", fontsize=10)
    ax.set_xlabel("value")
axes[0].set_ylabel("density")
plt.tight_layout()
```

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"What's the expected value of the maximum of $n$ uniform draws? The minimum?"* From
@eq-uniform-moments, $E[U_{(n)}]=n/(n+1)$ and $E[U_{(1)}]=1/(n+1)$ — beautifully symmetric
about $1/2$, and the maximum creeps toward $1$ as $n$ grows but never reaches it. If the
interviewer pushes to a general parent — *"and the expected maximum of $n$ Exponential(1)
draws?"* — do not quote a formula; derive it from @eq-max-cdf in three honest lines. For a
non-negative variable, the tail formula for the mean (itself a one-line consequence of
Fubini / swapping an integral and an expectation, [PROB-5]) gives
$E[X_{(n)}]=\int_0^\infty P(X_{(n)}>x)\,dx=\int_0^\infty\bigl(1-F(x)^n\bigr)\,dx$. For
Exponential(1), $F(x)=1-e^{-x}$, so substitute $t=1-e^{-x}$ (hence $x=-\ln(1-t)$ and
$dx=dt/(1-t)$; as $x:0\to\infty$, $t:0\to1$):
$$
E[X_{(n)}]=\int_0^1\frac{1-t^{\,n}}{1-t}\,dt
=\int_0^1\bigl(1+t+t^2+\cdots+t^{\,n-1}\bigr)\,dt
=\sum_{j=1}^{n}\frac{1}{j},
$$
using the finite geometric-series identity $\frac{1-t^n}{1-t}=\sum_{i=0}^{n-1}t^{\,i}$ and
integrating each power term. So $E[X_{(n)}]=H_n=\sum_{j=1}^n 1/j\approx\ln n+\gamma$ (the
harmonic number, with $\gamma\approx 0.577$ the Euler–Mascheroni constant): it grows
*without* bound, but only **logarithmically** — a clean illustration that the maximum's
behavior depends entirely on the tail of $F$ (the bounded uniform pins its max at $1$; the
exponential's lighter-than-uniform tail still lets the max wander off to infinity, just
slowly), which is the doorway to extreme-value theory flagged in @sec-failure.
:::

# Cashing it in, part two: why null p-values are uniform {#sec-pvalue}

Now the headline. We make precise what a p-value *is*, then read the PIT off it.

## What a p-value is, exactly {#sec-pvalue-def}

You run a test. You have a **test statistic** $T$ — a number computed from the data
designed so that larger values are stronger evidence against the null hypothesis $H_0$
(think of a $t$-statistic, a $\chi^2$, a difference in means). Before seeing data, $T$ is a
random variable; under the null it has some distribution with CDF $G(t)=P_{H_0}(T\le t)$.
You observe a particular value $t_{\text{obs}}$. The (one-sided, upper-tail) **p-value** is
the probability, *computed under the null*, of getting a test statistic at least as extreme
as the one you saw:
$$
p=P_{H_0}\bigl(T\ge t_{\text{obs}}\bigr)=1-G(t_{\text{obs}}).
$$ {#eq-pvalue-def}

That is the definition, no more: a p-value is a **null tail probability of the test
statistic**. Note what kind of object it is — it is the null CDF $G$ (well, its
complement) *evaluated at the random data*. That is precisely the shape $F(X)$ from the
PIT. The result is now one line away.

## The theorem {#sec-pvalue-thm}

::: {#thm-uniform-pvalue}
**Theorem (uniform null p-values).** Suppose the test statistic $T$ has a **continuous**
distribution under $H_0$, with CDF $G$. Then the p-value $P=1-G(T)$, viewed as a random
variable over repeated sampling *when $H_0$ is true*, is uniformly distributed on $(0,1)$:
$$
P_{H_0}(P\le \alpha)=\alpha
\qquad\text{for every } \alpha\in[0,1].
$$
:::

::: {.proof}
Work under $H_0$, so $T$ has CDF $G$. Apply the PIT (@thm-pit) to $T$: since $G$ is the
continuous CDF of $T$, the variable $G(T)$ is Uniform$(0,1)$. Now $P=1-G(T)$, and "one
minus a uniform" is again uniform — if $U\sim\mathrm{Uniform}(0,1)$ then so is $1-U$, by the
trivial symmetry $P(1-U\le \alpha)=P(U\ge 1-\alpha)=1-(1-\alpha)=\alpha$. Hence $P$ is
Uniform$(0,1)$, which is exactly the displayed statement $P_{H_0}(P\le\alpha)=\alpha$.
$\quad\blacksquare$
:::

Read what we just proved. The probability that a true null produces a p-value below the
threshold $\alpha$ is *exactly* $\alpha$. So the rule "reject when $p\le 0.05$" rejects a
true null with probability exactly $0.05$ — the test has the **Type I error rate it
advertises**. This is not a definition or a convention; it is forced by the PIT the moment
the test statistic is continuous. The entire promise of frequentist testing — "a level-$\alpha$
test has false-positive rate $\alpha$" — rests on this single transform.

::: {.callout-important title="Read the Core again"}
The order-statistics density (@eq-orderstat-density) and the uniform null p-value
(@thm-uniform-pvalue) are not two topics that happen to share an article. They are both
outputs of the probability integral transform: one collapses sorted samples onto Beta
variables, the other collapses null test statistics onto the uniform. *Same engine, two
exhausts.* That is the unification the Core promised.
:::

```{python}
#| label: fig-uniform-pvalues
#| fig-cap: "Null p-values are uniform — @thm-uniform-pvalue, simulated. We generate $50{,}000$ datasets under a *true* null (two groups of $n=20$ standard normals, no real difference) and run a two-sample $t$-test on each. **Left:** the histogram of the resulting p-values is flat across $(0,1)$, so each value is equally likely. **Right:** their empirical CDF lies on the diagonal $P(P\\le\\alpha)=\\alpha$ (red), confirming a level-$\\alpha$ test rejects exactly an $\\alpha$ fraction of true nulls. The dashed line marks $\\alpha=0.05$."
rng = np.random.default_rng(2024)
reps = 50_000
ngrp = 20
pvals = np.empty(reps)
for r in range(reps):
    a = rng.standard_normal(ngrp)
    b = rng.standard_normal(ngrp)   # SAME distribution: null is TRUE
    _, pvals[r] = stats.ttest_ind(a, b)

fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(8.8, 3.6))
axL.hist(pvals, bins=40, density=True, color=FILL, edgecolor="white")
axL.axhline(1.0, color=LINE, lw=2, label="Uniform(0,1)")
axL.set_xlabel("p-value"); axL.set_ylabel("density")
axL.set_title("Null p-values are flat"); axL.set_ylim(0, 1.5); axL.legend(fontsize=9)

grid = np.linspace(0, 1, 200)
ecdf = np.searchsorted(np.sort(pvals), grid, side="right") / reps
axR.plot(grid, ecdf, color=POINT, lw=2, label="empirical CDF of $p$")
axR.plot(grid, grid, color=LINE, lw=1.5, ls="-", label="diagonal $P(p\\le\\alpha)=\\alpha$")
axR.axvline(0.05, color=GREY, ls="--", lw=1.2)
axR.set_xlabel(r"$\alpha$"); axR.set_ylabel(r"$P(p\le\alpha)$")
axR.set_title("False-positive rate matches $\\alpha$"); axR.legend(fontsize=9, loc="lower right")
plt.tight_layout()
print(f"empirical P(p <= 0.05) under the null = {np.mean(pvals <= 0.05):.4f}  (target 0.0500)")
```

The printed false-positive rate lands right on $0.05$, the operational meaning of the
theorem.

## The payoff downstream: uniform order statistics meet null p-values {#sec-bh}

Here is where the two halves of the article fuse into one tool, and why the track points
this article at multiple-comparison procedures. Suppose you run $m$ hypothesis tests at
once — $m$ genes, $m$ ad variants, $m$ outcomes — and *all* the nulls are true. Then you
have $m$ i.i.d. Uniform$(0,1)$ p-values. Sort them: $p_{(1)}\le p_{(2)}\le\cdots\le
p_{(m)}$. By @thm-uniform-beta, the $k$-th smallest p-value is
$p_{(k)}\sim\mathrm{Beta}(k,m-k+1)$, with mean $k/(m+1)$. So *under the global null we know
exactly where each sorted p-value should sit* — that is the reference against which a
procedure decides which sorted p-values are "too small to be null." The Benjamini–Hochberg
false-discovery-rate procedure (Statistical Inference D5) compares the sorted $p_{(k)}$ to
the line $\alpha k/m$ for exactly this reason: the order statistics of the uniform are the
yardstick. @fig-bh shows the picture.

```{python}
#| label: fig-bh
#| fig-cap: "The bridge that powers multiple testing. Sorted p-values from $m=50$ tests, plotted against rank $k$. **Grey:** all nulls true — the points hug the diagonal $E[p_{(k)}]=k/(m+1)$ (dashed) exactly as the Beta law predicts, and stay above the Benjamini–Hochberg threshold line $\\alpha k/m$ (red, $\\alpha=0.1$), so nothing is (correctly) flagged. **Blue:** the same with 8 genuine effects injected — their small p-values peel below the threshold at the left, which is how a false-discovery-rate procedure detects them. Knowing the *null* order-statistic positions is what makes the detection possible."
rng = np.random.default_rng(5)
m = 50
alpha = 0.10

p_null = np.sort(rng.random(m))

p_mixed = rng.random(m)
p_mixed[:8] = rng.random(8) * 0.01   # 8 real effects -> tiny p-values
p_mixed = np.sort(p_mixed)

k = np.arange(1, m + 1)
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(7.0, 4.2))
ax.plot(k, p_null, "o", color=GREY, ms=4, label="all nulls true")
ax.plot(k, p_mixed, "o", color=POINT, ms=4, label="8 real effects mixed in")
ax.plot(k, k / (m + 1), color=ACCENT, ls="--", lw=1.5, label=r"null mean  $k/(m+1)$")
ax.plot(k, alpha * k / m, color=LINE, lw=1.8, label=r"BH threshold  $\alpha k/m$")
ax.set_xlabel("rank $k$"); ax.set_ylabel("sorted p-value $p_{(k)}$")
ax.set_ylim(0, 1.02)
ax.set_title("Sorted p-values vs. the uniform-null yardstick")
ax.legend(fontsize=8.5, loc="upper left")
plt.tight_layout()
```

# Worked examples {#sec-examples}

We now run the machinery on honest problems — one where it is the perfect tool, one near
the edge, and one where it quietly fails.

## Example 1 — flood engineering: the distribution of the annual maximum {#sec-flood}

A levee must withstand the largest river crest of the year. Suppose daily peak stage
(height) over the $n=365$ days of a year behaves like i.i.d. draws from a distribution $F$
(a real simplification — see the honesty note below — but a standard first model). The
quantity the engineer cares about is the **annual maximum** $X_{(365)}$, and its CDF is
@eq-max-cdf, $F_{(n)}(x)=F(x)^n$. For a "100-year flood" we want the level $x_{0.99}$ that
the annual max exceeds only 1% of years, i.e. $P(X_{(n)}> x)=0.01$, so
$F(x)^n=0.99\Rightarrow F(x)=0.99^{1/n}$. @fig-flood works it for an exponential-tailed
stage distribution.

```{python}
#| label: fig-flood
#| fig-cap: "Annual-maximum river stage, $n=365$ i.i.d. daily peaks from an Exponential(rate 1, in meters above baseline). **Left:** the density of a single day's peak (grey) versus the density of the *annual maximum* $X_{(365)}$ (red, from @eq-max-density) — the max lives far out in the tail, near 6 m, where any single day almost never reaches. **Right:** the annual-max CDF $F(x)^{365}$; the dashed lines read off the 100-year level (the $x$ where the curve hits $0.99$). Sorting is the entire content of a 'return level'."
from scipy.stats import expon

n = 365
xs = np.linspace(0, 9, 500)
f1 = expon.pdf(xs)
F1 = expon.cdf(xs)
f_max = n * F1**(n - 1) * f1
F_max = F1**n

x99 = expon.ppf(0.99**(1/n))   # 100-year level

fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(8.8, 3.6))
axL.plot(xs, f1, color=GREY, ls="--", label="single day")
axL.plot(xs, f_max, color=LINE, label="annual max $X_{(365)}$")
axL.set_xlabel("stage (m)"); axL.set_ylabel("density")
axL.set_title("One day vs. the year's worst day"); axL.legend(fontsize=9)

axR.plot(xs, F_max, color=POINT, lw=2)
axR.axhline(0.99, color=GREY, ls="--", lw=1.0)
axR.axvline(x99, color=LINE, ls="--", lw=1.2)
axR.annotate(f"100-yr level\n$x\\approx{x99:.2f}$ m", (x99, 0.5),
             textcoords="offset points", xytext=(-95, 0), fontsize=9, color=LINE)
axR.set_xlabel("stage (m)"); axR.set_ylabel("$P(\\mathrm{annual\\ max}\\le x)$")
axR.set_title("Annual-max CDF $F(x)^{365}$")
plt.tight_layout()
print(f"100-year flood level (annual-max 99th pct) = {x99:.3f} m")
print(f"single-day prob of exceeding that level    = {1-expon.cdf(x99):.2e}")
```

The single-day exceedance probability is minuscule, yet over 365 chances the annual maximum
routinely reaches it — the arithmetic of $F(x)^n$ made physical. This is exactly the regime
order statistics were invented for, and the computation is honest and clean.

::: {.callout-warning title="Pitfall"}
**The i.i.d. assumption here is a real simplification, and you should say so.** Daily river
stages are *not* independent (wet weeks cluster) nor identically distributed (snowmelt
seasons differ), so $F(x)^{365}$ is a first approximation, not gospel. The serious tool for
maxima — and the honest edge of this article — is **extreme-value theory**, which we flag
in @sec-failure: under broad conditions the *distribution of the maximum itself* converges
to one of three shapes (Gumbel, Fréchet, Weibull), and engineers fit those directly to
annual maxima rather than trusting a daily $F$.
:::

## Example 2 — calibration on the diagonal: the uniform Q–Q plot {#sec-qq}

A practitioner's most frequent encounter with this article's machinery is the **Q–Q plot**
(quantile–quantile plot), and the cleanest version asks: *are these numbers uniform?* —
which, after a PIT, is the question *is this distribution what I think it is?* The recipe
falls straight out of @eq-uniform-moments: if $U_{(1)}\le\cdots\le U_{(n)}$ are uniform
order statistics, then $E[U_{(k)}]=k/(n+1)$, so we plot the sorted data against the
theoretical positions $k/(n+1)$ and check for the diagonal.

We use this twice: once on genuinely uniform data (it lies on the line), once to *diagnose*
a test whose statistic distribution we got wrong (it bends off the line) — the everyday
honest use.

```{python}
#| label: fig-qq
#| fig-cap: "Uniform Q–Q plots, the practitioner's calibration check. Sorted values plotted against the order-statistic means $k/(n+1)$ from @eq-uniform-moments. **Left:** $n=200$ genuinely Uniform(0,1) values — the points track the diagonal, calibration confirmed. **Right:** p-values from a mis-specified test (we wrongly used a $z$-test when the true null statistic is heavier-tailed $t_3$); the systematic bow *off* the diagonal is the visual signature of a miscalibrated test that would not control its error rate. The Q–Q plot turns @thm-uniform-pvalue into a diagnostic you can see."
from scipy.stats import t as t_dist, norm as norm_dist

rng = np.random.default_rng(99)
n = 200
positions = np.arange(1, n + 1) / (n + 1)

good = np.sort(rng.random(n))

# A test whose statistic is really t_3 but analyzed as if standard normal:
stat = t_dist.rvs(df=3, size=n, random_state=rng)
p_wrong = 1 - norm_dist.cdf(stat)        # WRONG null CDF -> miscalibrated p-values
p_wrong = np.sort(p_wrong)

fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(8.8, 3.7))
for ax, data, ttl in [(axL, good, "Genuinely uniform"),
                      (axR, p_wrong, "Miscalibrated test")]:
    ax.plot([0, 1], [0, 1], color=LINE, lw=1.5)
    ax.scatter(positions, data, color=POINT, s=14, zorder=3)
    ax.set_xlabel("theoretical position $k/(n+1)$")
    ax.set_ylabel("sorted value")
    ax.set_title(ttl)
plt.tight_layout()
```

The right panel is the honest applied payoff: a test that *looks* fine until you plot its
null p-values against the uniform reference and watch them peel off the diagonal. That
bow is a miscalibrated false-positive rate, invisible in any single p-value but obvious in
the order statistics.

## Example 3 — the edge: a discrete test, where uniformity breaks {#sec-discrete-example}

Now the dishonest-looking case that is in fact the most instructive. The PIT — and hence
the uniform-p-value theorem — needs a **continuous** test statistic. Many real tests are
**discrete**: Fisher's exact test, a binomial test, a permutation test on a small sample.
Their statistics take finitely many values, so $G$ has jumps, $G(T)$ can only land on those
jump heights, and the p-value is **not** uniform — it is *conservative* (stochastically
larger than uniform), meaning the true Type I error is *below* the nominal $\alpha$.
@fig-discrete shows it.

```{python}
#| label: fig-discrete
#| fig-cap: "The edge of applicability: discrete tests do NOT give uniform null p-values. $30{,}000$ binomial tests of $H_0: p=0.5$ with $n=20$ Bernoulli trials, simulated under the *true* null. **Left:** the p-value distribution is a staircase, not flat — it can only take the discrete values the binomial tail allows. **Right:** its empirical CDF lies *below* the diagonal, so $P(p\\le 0.05)<0.05$: the test is conservative, rejecting *less* often than advertised. The continuity hypothesis of @thm-uniform-pvalue is not a technicality — drop it and uniformity is gone."
from scipy.stats import binomtest

rng = np.random.default_rng(7)
reps = 30_000
n = 20
pvals = np.empty(reps)
for r in range(reps):
    successes = rng.binomial(n, 0.5)             # null is TRUE
    pvals[r] = binomtest(successes, n, 0.5, alternative="two-sided").pvalue

fig, (axL, axR) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(8.8, 3.6))
axL.hist(pvals, bins=40, density=True, color=FILL, edgecolor="white")
axL.axhline(1.0, color=LINE, lw=2, label="uniform (continuous case)")
axL.set_xlabel("p-value"); axL.set_ylabel("density")
axL.set_title("Discrete test: a staircase"); axL.legend(fontsize=9)

grid = np.linspace(0, 1, 200)
ecdf = np.searchsorted(np.sort(pvals), grid, side="right") / reps
axR.plot(grid, ecdf, color=POINT, lw=2, label="empirical CDF")
axR.plot(grid, grid, color=LINE, lw=1.5, label="diagonal (would-be uniform)")
axR.set_xlabel(r"$\alpha$"); axR.set_ylabel(r"$P(p\le\alpha)$")
axR.set_title("Conservative: CDF below the diagonal"); axR.legend(fontsize=9, loc="lower right")
plt.tight_layout()
print(f"empirical P(p <= 0.05) for discrete test = {np.mean(pvals <= 0.05):.4f}  (< 0.05: conservative)")
```

The empirical rejection rate sits *below* $0.05$: the discrete test throws away power to
stay valid. The honest fix is the **mid-p-value** or a randomized test, which restores
uniformity in expectation; either way, the lesson is that the clean theorem is exactly as
strong as its continuity hypothesis, no stronger.

# Failure modes and the edge of applicability {#sec-failure}

This is the section that separates "can write the density" from "knows when it lies."

## Ties and discreteness break everything cleanly {#sec-ties}

The whole derivation assumed a **continuous** parent so that ties have probability zero and
the PIT holds exactly. With discrete data:

- **The order-statistic density does not exist** (there is no density at all); you work
  with the order-statistic *pmf*, and the clean multinomial product picks up correction
  terms for tied values.
- **Null p-values are not uniform** but conservative (@sec-discrete-example), so naive
  multiple-testing corrections built on the uniform yardstick are themselves conservative.

The reach-for-instead: exact discrete combinatorics, mid-p adjustments, or — increasingly —
permutation/Monte-Carlo p-values that are *continuous by construction* (they are
themselves uniform-on-a-grid and approach true uniformity as the number of permutations
grows), which is why resampling tests (built on [PROB-22]) are the modern default when
continuity is in doubt.

## Dependence destroys the simple formulas {#sec-dependence}

Every result here assumed **i.i.d.** Drop independence and the binomial count
@eq-binomial-count is wrong (the indicators $B_i$ are no longer independent), the joint
$n!\prod f$ of @eq-joint-density is wrong, and the maximum's $F(x)^n$ is wrong. Real
time-series maxima (the flood example) and spatially correlated data violate this. The
order statistics still exist and still sort; what fails is the *distribution theory* — you
lose the closed forms and must simulate or use dependence-aware extreme-value models.

## Extreme order statistics have non-normal limits {#sec-evt}

A natural but wrong instinct, fresh off the central limit theorem, is to expect
$X_{(n)}$ to be asymptotically normal. It is not. The sample **maximum**, suitably
centered and scaled, converges not to a Gaussian but to one of exactly three
**extreme-value** laws — Gumbel, Fréchet, or Weibull — depending only on the *tail* of
$F$ (the Fisher–Tippett–Gnedenko theorem). This is the honest boundary the track flags but
does not build: the *middle* order statistics (sample quantiles) are asymptotically normal
and behave as your CLT intuition expects, but the *extremes* obey a different limit theory
entirely. If your question is genuinely about the tail — worst loss, record flood, maximum
load — reach for extreme-value theory, not for normal approximations.

::: {.callout-tip title="You might be asked"}
*"When is the order-statistics / uniform-p-value machinery the right tool, and when is it
the wrong one?"* The recitable summary:

- **Use it** when (a) the data or the test statistic is **continuous**, (b) the sample is
  **i.i.d.** (or close enough that the dependence is mild), and (c) you care about a
  *specific rank* — a quantile, the median, the max — or about *calibrating / combining*
  p-values (Q–Q plots, false-discovery control). In that regime it is exact, closed-form,
  and beautiful: Beta order statistics, uniform null p-values, the lot.
- **Do not lean on it** when the statistic is **discrete** (p-values go conservative — use
  mid-p or permutation tests), when the data are strongly **dependent** (the closed forms
  fail — simulate), or when you are chasing the **far tail** of the maximum/minimum
  (use extreme-value theory, not the i.i.d. $F^n$ extrapolated naively).
- **The deepest caveat:** a uniform p-value is a statement about *frequencies under the
  null over repeated samples*. It says nothing about the probability that *this* null is
  true given *this* p-value — that is a Bayesian posterior, a different object entirely
  ([INFR E4]). Confusing the two is the classic interview trap and the source of most
  p-value abuse.
:::

# A loose end, tied off: the telescoping derivative {#sec-telescoping}

We promised in @sec-density that differentiating the CDF @eq-orderstat-cdf gives the same
density as the counting argument. Here is the cancellation in full, for the reader who wants
no gap. Write $G(x)=\sum_{j=k}^{n}\binom{n}{j}F^{j}(1-F)^{\,n-j}$ with $F=F(x)$, and
differentiate using the product and chain rules, with $F'=f$:
$$
G'(x)=\sum_{j=k}^{n}\binom{n}{j}\Bigl[\,j F^{j-1}(1-F)^{n-j}
       - (n-j)F^{j}(1-F)^{\,n-j-1}\Bigr]f.
$$
Use the identities $\binom{n}{j} j = n\binom{n-1}{j-1}$ and $\binom{n}{j}(n-j)=n\binom{n-1}{j}$
(each is one line of factorial algebra: $\binom{n}{j}j=\frac{n!}{(j-1)!(n-j)!}=n\frac{(n-1)!}{(j-1)!(n-j)!}=n\binom{n-1}{j-1}$,
and similarly for the second). Then
$$
G'(x)=n f\sum_{j=k}^{n}\Bigl[\binom{n-1}{j-1}F^{j-1}(1-F)^{n-j}
        - \binom{n-1}{j}F^{j}(1-F)^{\,n-1-j}\Bigr].
$$
Re-index the first sum with $i=j-1$ (so $i$ runs $k-1$ to $n-1$) and leave the second with
index $j$ (running $k$ to $n-1$, since the $j=n$ term has $\binom{n-1}{n}=0$). Both sums now
have the identical summand $\binom{n-1}{\cdot}F^{\cdot}(1-F)^{\,n-1-\cdot}$, so this is a
**telescoping difference**: every term of the second sum cancels a term of the first,
leaving only the first sum's earliest term ($i=k-1$):
$$
G'(x)=n f\,\binom{n-1}{k-1}F^{\,k-1}(1-F)^{\,n-k}
=\frac{n!}{(k-1)!\,(n-k)!}\,F^{\,k-1}(1-F)^{\,n-k}\,f,
$$
using $n\binom{n-1}{k-1}=\frac{n!}{(k-1)!(n-k)!}$. This is identical to
@eq-orderstat-density. The two derivations agree exactly, as the "You might be asked" box
in @sec-density claimed — claim discharged, no hand-waving. $\quad\blacksquare$

# Connections and where to go next {#sec-connections}

::: {.callout-important title="Where you are in the track"}
You arrived at the **end of the Probability Foundations track**. This article consumed the
counting of **[PROB-10]** (the multinomial coefficient), the densities of **[PROB-4]**, the
Beta distribution of **[PROB-14]**, and the i.i.d. sample of **[PROB-8]**, and it is the
sibling of **[PROB-22 *The Empirical CDF*]** — together they are the two "objects sampling
theory is built from" that the data tracks consume. From here the threads run outward, into
other tracks:

- **Statistical Inference D5 (multiple comparisons)** consumes the headline pairing of this
  article — uniform null p-values plus their Beta order statistics — to build
  Bonferroni, Benjamini–Hochberg, and the false-discovery-rate machinery (@sec-bh).
- **The bootstrap (INFR E1)**, resting on the empirical CDF of [PROB-22], uses
  order-statistic-based **percentile confidence intervals** — the $\alpha/2$ and
  $1-\alpha/2$ order statistics of the resampled estimates.
- **Q–Q plots and goodness-of-fit** (INFR, and every applied track) are the
  order-statistic-versus-quantile picture of @sec-qq, generalized from the uniform to any
  reference distribution via the PIT.
- **Extreme-value theory** (flagged in @sec-evt) is the proper home of the maximum and
  minimum when the tail is what matters — the rabbit hole this article deliberately opened
  but did not descend.
:::

# Going deeper {#sec-deeper}

- **Primary sources.** H. A. David and H. N. Nagaraja, *Order Statistics* (3rd ed., 2003)
  is the canonical reference for everything in @sec-formal, including the discrete and
  dependent cases this article only flagged. For the p-value uniformity in its testing
  context, J. Neyman and E. S. Pearson's 1933 paper on the theory of testing and R. A.
  Fisher's *Statistical Methods for Research Workers* are the origin.
- **The multiple-testing payoff.** Y. Benjamini and Y. Hochberg, "Controlling the False
  Discovery Rate," *JRSS-B* 57 (1995): the procedure of @sec-bh, built directly on the
  uniform order statistics derived here.
- **The extreme-value rabbit hole.** S. Coles, *An Introduction to Statistical Modeling of
  Extreme Values* (2001), for the Fisher–Tippett–Gnedenko trichotomy behind @sec-evt — the
  honest tool for the flood problem of @sec-flood.
- **Open threads deliberately deferred.** *What is the joint distribution of two order
  statistics* (needed for the variance of a sample range or interquartile range)? — a
  two-dimensional version of the counting argument in @sec-density. *How fast do sample
  quantiles converge, and to what?* — the Bahadur representation and the asymptotic
  normality of central order statistics, which sit at the boundary with the convergence
  theory of [PROB-21].
